rows.v: Thin, wispy, feathery streaks high in the sky — the highest of the common clouds, made entirely of ice crystals.
Forms above ~6,000 m where the air is freezing cold. Ice crystals fall and get swept sideways by high winds into those delicate 'mare's tails.'
Often the first sign that a warm front and rain are approaching within a day or so. On their own, they mean fair but changing weather.
Delicate white filaments or hooks, very high up, that don't block the sun. No shading underneath.
A thin, milky veil of high cloud that covers the whole sky and often produces a halo around the sun or moon.
A sheet of ice crystals spreading ahead of an advancing warm front, thin enough that the sun still shines through.
A reliable sign rain or snow is likely within 12–24 hours as the front approaches.
Whitish sky-wide haze with a ring (halo) around the sun or moon — the giveaway feature.
Small, white, rippled patches high in the sky — the classic 'mackerel sky' that looks like fish scales.
Ice crystals arranged in tiny regular cells by gentle turbulence at high altitude. Short-lived, often changing into other cloud types.
Folklore says 'mackerel sky, never long dry' — often a sign of changing, unsettled weather ahead.
Rows of tiny white ripples or grains, no shading, very high. Like fish scales across the sky.
The straight white lines aircraft leave behind — artificial clouds made of ice crystals from engine exhaust.
Hot, moist jet exhaust meets very cold high-altitude air and the water vapor freezes instantly into a line of ice crystals.
How long they last hints at high-altitude humidity: short-lived means dry air, long-lasting and spreading means moist air and possible weather change.
Perfectly straight lines following flight paths, unlike any natural cloud. Spreading ones can blur into cirrus.
A gray or blue-gray sheet covering much of the sky, through which the sun appears as a dim, watery disc.
A uniform mid-level layer of water droplets and ice, thickening ahead of a warm front or large weather system.
Steady, widespread rain or snow often follows within hours as it thickens and lowers into nimbostratus.
The 'frosted glass' look — sun visible as a faint glow with no halo and no sharp shadows on the ground.
Mid-level patches or rolls of white and gray cloud, often in groups or waves across part of the sky.
Water droplets organized into cells by convection and wind at medium height, frequently in distinctive rows.
On a warm, humid morning, altocumulus can warn of afternoon thunderstorms. Otherwise it signals fair but unsettled air.
Mid-sized cloud blobs in groups, each with some shading, often in rippled bands. Bigger than cirrocumulus, higher than stratocumulus.
Smooth, lens- or saucer-shaped clouds that hover near mountains — the ones often mistaken for UFOs.
Moist air flowing over a mountain rises and cools in a standing wave, condensing into a stationary lens shape at the wave's crest.
Indicate strong winds and atmospheric waves aloft — a warning sign for pilots of turbulence near mountains.
A stack of smooth, stationary 'flying saucer' discs downwind of a mountain peak, holding still while other clouds move.
A low, gray, featureless blanket covering the whole sky — essentially fog that didn't touch the ground.
A shallow layer of cloud forms when a large body of cool, moist air is lifted gently or mist rises slightly off the surface.
Means dull, overcast, often drizzly weather. Usually no heavy rain, just gloom and possible mist.
Flat, uniform gray with no distinct shapes and no visible sun. Lowest of the layered clouds.
Low, lumpy gray-and-white cloud arranged in patches, rolls, or a broken honeycomb across the sky.
A low layer broken into cells by weak convection — the most common cloud type on Earth, especially over oceans.
Usually means dry, settled weather; rarely produces more than light drizzle. Often clears by afternoon.
Low, clumpy rolls with darker bases and gaps of blue or lighter sky between them.
The classic puffy 'cotton ball' or 'cauliflower' cloud with a flat base and bright white rounded top.
Rising bubbles of warm air (thermals) cool as they ascend until their moisture condenses, building a fluffy heap.
Small, scattered cumulus on a sunny day means fair weather. If they grow tall through the afternoon, storms may follow.
Detached white puffs with flat gray bottoms and bright bumpy tops, drifting across blue sky.
Cloud at ground level — a suspension of tiny water droplets that cuts visibility to under a kilometer.
Forms when moist air cools to its dew point near the surface: overnight cooling (radiation fog) or warm air over a cold surface (advection fog).
Signals very humid, calm conditions. Usually burns off as the sun warms the ground, but can be a serious driving hazard.
A gray veil at eye level reducing visibility, often densest in valleys and near water at dawn.
The thick, dark gray rain cloud — a featureless, sky-covering layer that delivers steady, prolonged precipitation.
A deep, moisture-laden layer that thickens and lowers from altostratus as a warm front arrives, raining continuously.
Means hours of steady, soaking rain or snow — not the brief downpour of a thunderstorm, but the all-day kind.
Uniformly dark, low, and wet-looking, with no structure and falling rain or snow blurring its base.
The king of clouds — a towering thundercloud that can reach the top of the troposphere, bringing storms.
A cumulus cloud fed by strong updrafts grows explosively upward until it hits the stratosphere and flattens into an anvil.
Means imminent severe weather: heavy rain, lightning, hail, strong winds, and possibly tornadoes.
A huge, dark-based tower with a flat, spreading 'anvil' top. Often the only cloud that matters when you see one.
A cumulus cloud that has grown tall and cauliflower-like — the stage just before it becomes a thunderstorm.
Vigorous updrafts push a fair-weather cumulus sharply upward, its top hardening into firm, sprouting domes.
A warning that the atmosphere is unstable and full thunderstorms (cumulonimbus) may develop within the hour.
Cumulus that's clearly taller than it is wide, with crisp, surging tops but not yet an anvil.
Strange pouches or lobes hanging down from the underside of a cloud, usually a thunderstorm's anvil.
Pockets of cooler, sinking air sag below the cloud base, forming the distinctive drooping bubbles.
Often appear with or after severe thunderstorms — a sign of a turbulent, energetic storm nearby.
Rows of smooth, hanging pouches under a dark cloud, often lit dramatically near sunset.
A low, wedge-shaped cloud on the leading edge of a thunderstorm, looking like an advancing shelf or ledge.
Cool air rushing out ahead of a storm (the outflow) lifts warm moist air, condensing it into a long horizontal wedge.
Marks the gust front — strong, sudden winds and heavy rain arrive right behind it.
A long, low, smooth horizontal ledge stretching across the sky ahead of a dark storm, often rising and ragged underneath.
Liquid water droplets large enough to fall from cloud to ground — the most common form of precipitation.
Cloud droplets collide and merge (or melt from ice) until they're heavy enough to overcome the updraft and fall.
The type and intensity reveal the weather system: steady rain from fronts, sudden downpours from convective storms.
Falling drops you can see and feel; steady from layered cloud, bursty from towering cloud.
Ice crystals that fall as flakes when the whole atmosphere is below freezing.
Water vapor freezes directly onto ice crystals in the cloud, which clump into flakes and fall without melting.
Tells you the air column is at or below freezing top to bottom. Wet snow is near 0°C; powder is much colder.
White flakes; large and sticky when near freezing, small and dry/powdery when very cold.
Balls or lumps of ice that fall from strong thunderstorms, ranging from pea-sized to grapefruit-sized.
Ice is carried up and down repeatedly by violent updrafts, gaining a new frozen layer each trip until too heavy to hold aloft.
A marker of a powerful storm with strong updrafts. Large hail signals a severe, possibly tornadic storm.
Solid ice pellets bouncing on the ground, often with visible layers if you cut one open.
Small, translucent ice pellets that bounce when they hit the ground — frozen raindrops.
Snow melts falling through a warm layer, then refreezes into ice pellets passing through cold air near the surface.
Indicates a shallow warm layer aloft over freezing air below — a classic winter-storm temperature profile.
Tiny clear ice balls that bounce and rattle on hard surfaces, unlike soft snow.
Rain that falls as liquid but freezes on contact with cold surfaces, coating everything in clear ice.
Snow melts in a warm layer and the raindrops stay liquid but supercooled, freezing instantly when they touch a sub-freezing surface.
The most dangerous winter precipitation — it glazes roads, trees, and power lines with ice.
Wet-looking rain that turns into a smooth, clear ice coating on cars, branches, and pavement.
Very fine, light rain made of tiny droplets that seem to float down slowly.
Falls from low stratus cloud where droplets are too small to grow into proper raindrops.
Signals a moist, stable, overcast airmass — gentle and persistent rather than heavy.
Mist-like droplets so fine they barely make a sound, often with low gray cloud or fog.
A storm from a cumulonimbus cloud, producing lightning, thunder, heavy rain, and often gusty wind.
Strong updrafts build a tall thundercloud; charge separates within it until it discharges as lightning, heating air into thunder.
Means heavy rain, lightning danger, and possibly hail or damaging wind for a short, intense period.
Dark towering cloud, rumbling thunder, sudden heavy rain and wind; lightning flashes the giveaway.
The most dangerous kind of thunderstorm, organized around a deep, rotating updraft called a mesocyclone.
Wind that changes speed and direction with height tilts the updraft into a long-lived rotating column, sustaining the storm for hours.
Produces the most violent weather: giant hail, destructive winds, and the majority of strong tornadoes.
A single dominant storm, often with a rotating wall cloud, a hard 'overshooting' top, and a striated, sculpted look.
A violently rotating column of air reaching from a thunderstorm cloud down to the ground.
A storm's rotating mesocyclone tightens and stretches toward the surface, accelerating the spin into a destructive vortex.
Among the most destructive weather on Earth, with winds that can exceed 300 km/h in the strongest cases.
A funnel or rope of cloud and debris connecting cloud to ground, often with a roaring sound and dark, rotating sky.
A massive rotating tropical storm with sustained winds over 119 km/h, also called a typhoon or cyclone.
Warm ocean water (above ~26°C) feeds rising moist air; the Earth's rotation organizes it into a spiraling system with a calm eye.
Brings days of destructive wind, torrential rain, and dangerous storm surge along coasts.
On satellite, a huge spiral with a clear central eye; on the ground, prolonged extreme wind and rain in bands.
A massive electrical discharge between cloud and ground, or within clouds, hotter than the sun's surface.
Charge separates as ice and water collide in a storm cloud until the difference is great enough to leap as a giant spark.
Always means a thunderstorm is present and you are at risk if outdoors — even before the rain arrives.
A brilliant branching flash followed by thunder; count the gap in seconds and divide by three for distance in km.
A widespread, fast-moving windstorm produced by a long line of intense thunderstorms.
A bow-shaped line of storms generates a continuous wall of strong straight-line winds that races across hundreds of kilometers.
Causes tornado-like wind damage over a huge area without any rotation — a long, destructive blast of wind.
A long, advancing shelf cloud and a sudden, sustained roar of damaging straight-line wind across a wide region.
An arc of spectral color formed when sunlight passes through raindrops opposite the sun.
Light enters each raindrop, bends (refracts), reflects off the back, and bends again, splitting into colors at fixed angles.
Tells you there's both rain and sunshine at once — the sun behind you, a shower ahead.
A colored arc opposite the sun, red on the outside, violet inside; a fainter secondary bow may appear above it.
A ring of light around the sun or moon, caused by ice crystals high in the atmosphere.
Light refracts through six-sided ice crystals in cirrostratus cloud, bending at a consistent 22° to form a ring.
Often a sign of an approaching warm front and rain within a day — 'ring around the moon, rain soon.'
A bright circle at a fixed distance from the sun or moon, sometimes faintly rainbow-tinted, with thin high cloud.
Bright, often colorful spots that appear to the left and right of the sun, at the same height.
Flat, hexagonal ice crystals drifting horizontally refract sunlight to either side of the sun at 22°.
Like halos, indicates high ice cloud and sometimes approaching weather. Also called mock suns or parhelia.
A pair of luminous, sometimes rainbow-edged patches flanking the sun, brightest when the sun is low.
Shimmering curtains of green, red, or purple light in the night sky near the poles — the Northern/Southern Lights.
Charged particles from the sun funnel along Earth's magnetic field and collide with gases high in the atmosphere, making them glow.
Tells you a burst of solar activity (a geomagnetic storm) is hitting Earth's magnetic field.
Moving, glowing bands or rays of color in a dark polar sky, strongest after intense solar storms.
Sunbeams that appear to radiate from the sun through gaps in cloud — 'God rays.'
Sunlight streams through breaks in clouds and is scattered by dust and haze, making the individual beams visible.
Mostly a beautiful optical effect; they mark broken cloud and scattered light rather than a forecast.
Distinct shafts of light fanning out from behind a cloud or below the horizon, especially near sunrise or sunset.
The leading edge of an advancing mass of cold air, pushing under and lifting the warm air ahead of it.
Dense cold air wedges beneath lighter warm air, forcing it up steeply and rapidly, often triggering towering clouds.
Brings a sharp change: brief heavy showers or thunderstorms, then clearing skies, falling temperatures, and gusty winds.
A line of towering clouds and showers passing through, followed by cooler, drier, clearer air and a wind shift.
The leading edge of advancing warm air, gliding up and over the cooler air it's replacing.
Warm air rises gently over a wedge of cooler air, creating a long, sloping ramp of progressively lower cloud.
Brings a slow sequence — high cirrus, then thickening cloud, then prolonged steady rain — followed by milder air.
A gradual lowering and thickening of cloud over a day, then steady rain, ending with warmer, more humid conditions.
A region where air sinks and spreads out at the surface, generally bringing calm, settled weather.
Descending air warms and dries, suppressing cloud formation. Winds circle it clockwise in the Northern Hemisphere.
Usually means fair, dry, stable weather — sunny in summer, but clear cold nights and possible fog in winter.
On a map, an 'H' with widely spaced isobars; in the sky, clear or fair-weather conditions and light winds.
A region where air rises and converges, generally bringing clouds, wind, and unsettled weather.
Rising air cools and condenses into cloud and precipitation. Winds spiral inward, counterclockwise in the Northern Hemisphere.
Means changeable, often wet and windy weather, and is where fronts and storms organize.
On a map, an 'L' with tightly packed isobars; in the sky, increasing cloud, wind, and rain.
A fast, narrow ribbon of wind high in the atmosphere that steers weather systems around the globe.
Strong temperature contrasts between air masses create a band of very high-speed winds near the top of the troposphere.
Its position determines where storms track and whether you get warm or cold spells — the master switch of mid-latitude weather.
Invisible from the ground, but its influence shows as the path storms follow; pilots feel it as high-altitude wind.
